You are an AI assistant that completes tasks by reasoning and using tools.

## Think Before Acting

Before making tool calls, briefly explain your reasoning in 1-3 sentences:
- What you learned from the previous step
- What you're doing next and why

Don't over-explain. Be concise but show your thinking.

## Tools

**Always Available (Meta-Tools):**
- `todo_write` - Task planning: create/update todos. Takes `todos` array [{id, content, status}] and `merge` boolean.
- `toolbelt_list_tools` / `toolbelt_inspect_tool` / `toolbelt_add_tool` / `toolbelt_remove_tool` - Tool management
- `final_answer` - Submit your answer (status: completed/blocked/failed)

**Domain Tools:** Use `toolbelt_list_tools` to discover, then `toolbelt_add_tool` to add them.

## Workflow

1. Plan: Use `todo_write` to create todos for complex tasks
2. Discover: Use `toolbelt_list_tools` to find relevant tools
3. Execute: Work through todos, use `todo_write` with `merge=true` to update status
4. Complete: Call `final_answer` (all todos must be completed/cancelled first)

## Rules

- Update todo status with `todo_write`: set `in_progress` when starting, `completed` when done
- Show your work for calculations
- `final_answer` is rejected if todos are incomplete

Susan Whitaker has filed three successive Complaints against Magnolia Gardens. The first is the original Complaint, the second Is the First Amended Complaint, and the third is the Second Amended Complaint. Each Complaint was served on the defendant ten days after they were filed. Magnolia Gardens wants to file a Motion to Dismiss the breach of contract allegations in the Complaint. 

Review the complaints and decide if, under Texas Rule of Civil Procedure 91a.3, what is the last day the motion to dismiss can be timely filed? When was the original complaint served?
Give me your answer right here.

CAUSE NO. D-1-GN-25-004732
IN THE DISTRICT COURT
TRAVIS COUNTY, TEXAS
53rd JUDICIAL DISTRICT
SUSAN WHITAKER, individually and on behalf of her father, Thomas Whitaker
Plaintiff,
v.
MAGNOLIA GARDENS, INC. d/b/a THE GROVE–AUSTIN
Defendant.
PLAINTIFF’S ORIGINAL COMPLAINT FOR BREACH OF CONTRACT


[page 2]
I. INTRODUCTION
Plaintiff brings this action against Defendant Magnolia Gardens, Inc. for breach of contract arising
from Defendant’s failure to provide the level of staffing, nutrition, and resident enrichment services
expressly promised in its Residential Care Agreement and related materials. Defendant unilaterally
reduced services and quality of care while continuing to charge residents full rates and represent that its
standards remained unchanged.
II. PARTIES AND JURISDICTION
1. Plaintiff Susan Whitaker is a resident of Travis County, Texas, and entered into the Residential Care
Agreement on behalf of her father, Thomas Whitaker, for tenancy and care at The Grove–Austin.
2. Defendant Magnolia Gardens, Inc. (“Magnolia”) is an Illinois corporation headquartered at 500
North Michigan Avenue, Suite 3200, Chicago, IL 60611, and operates The Grove–Austin facility at
2900 Riverside Drive, Austin, Texas 78704.
3. Jurisdiction and venue are proper in this Court because the acts and omissions giving rise to this
claim occurred in Travis County, Texas.


[page 3]
III. CONTRACTUAL PROVISIONS
The Residential Care Agreement and related materials between Plaintiff and Defendant included the
following express provisions:
Provision 1 – Staffing Ratio and Care Standards: “Magnolia Gardens shall maintain an average
staff-to-resident ratio of eight-to-one (8:1) at all times, ensuring adequate supervision and assistance for
all residents consistent with state regulations and the resident’s individual care plan.”
Provision 2 – Nutrition and Meal Service: “Magnolia Gardens shall provide three nutritionally
balanced, freshly prepared meals each day, including breakfast, lunch, and dinner, suitable to each
resident’s dietary needs.”
Provision 3 – Wellness and Enrichment Programming: “Magnolia Gardens shall offer daily
wellness and enrichment programs designed to promote residents’ mental, physical, and spiritual
health.”
"},"search":null}

[body.p.4] (Heading 2): PLAINTIFF’S SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT FOR BREACH OF CONTRACT

[body.p.5] (normal): 
Filed: February 14, 2026


[body.p.6] (Heading 2): I. INTRODUCTION

[body.p.7] (normal): Plaintiff files this Second Amended Complaint to refine and clarify factual allegations regarding Defendant’s material breaches of the 2023 Residential Care Agreement governing the assisted living placement of Thomas Whitaker at The Grove–Austin. Plaintiff continues to bring a single cause of action for breach of contract.

[body.p.8] (Heading 2): II. PARTIES, JURISDICTION, AND VENUE

[body.p.9] (normal): 1. Plaintiff Susan Whitaker is an individual residing in Travis County, Texas. Plaintiff entered into the 2023 Residential Care Agreement on behalf of her father, Thomas Whitaker.

[body.p.10] (normal): 2. Defendant Magnolia Gardens, Inc. is a Delaware corporation headquartered at 500 North Michigan Avenue, Suite 3200, Chicago, Illinois 60611. Defendant operates The Grove–Austin at 2900 Riverside Drive, Austin, Texas 78704.

[body.p.11] (normal): 3. Venue and jurisdiction are proper in this Court because the contract was executed, performed, and breached in Travis County, Texas.

[body.p.12] (Heading 2): III. CONTRACTUAL OBLIGATIONS

[body.p.13] (normal): Under the 2023 Residential Care Agreement, Defendant undertook several material obligations, including but not limited to the following:

[body.p.14] (normal): • Maintaining a resident-to-staff ratio not to exceed eight-to-one (8:1).

[body.p.15] (normal): • Conducting comprehensive Care Plan assessments upon admission and every three (3) months thereafter.

[body.p.16] (normal): • Providing three nutritionally balanced meals each day.

[body.p.17] (normal): • Offering a monthly calendar of regular social, recreational, and wellness activities.

[body.p.18] (normal): • Delivering all services outlined in the individualized Care Plan.

[body.p.19] (normal): • Providing at least sixty (60) days’ written notice prior to any material change affecting staffing, services, activities, or fees.

[body.p.20] (Heading 2): IV. FACTUAL ALLEGATIONS

[body.p.21] (normal): 4. In or around February 2025, Defendant implemented a series of operational changes at The Grove–Austin affecting staffing, meals, reassessments, activity offerings, and performance of Care Plan items.

[body.p.22] (normal): 5. Following these changes, Plaintiff observed and received reports of delays in essential care services due to insufficient staffing, particularly during early morning and evening hours.

[body.p.23] (normal): 6. Around mid-February 2025, breakfast service was reduced from a prepared hot meal to a limited continental offering without prior written notice to residents or their families.

[body.p.24] (normal): 7. Beginning in March 2025, the number and frequency of scheduled activities decreased substantially, contrary to the Agreement’s assurances regarding social and recreational programming.

[body.p.25] (normal): 8. Plaintiff later discovered that Thomas Whitaker’s required quarterly reassessment for 2025 had not taken place within the contractually mandated timeframe.

[body.p.26] (normal): 9. During this period, core Care Plan services—such as mobility assistance and timely medication reminders—were not consistently provided.

[body.p.27] (normal): 10. Despite these material changes, Defendant did not provide the sixty (60) days’ written notice required under the Agreement.

[body.p.28] (normal): 11. Plaintiff continued to pay all required fees, except for a temporary rate adjustment implemented by Defendant in May 2025.

[body.p.29] (normal): 12. Defendant’s actions deprived Plaintiff and her father of the benefit of their bargain under the Agreement.

[body.p.30] (Heading 2): V. CAUSE OF ACTION — BREACH OF CONTRACT

[body.p.31] (normal): 13. Plaintiff incorporates paragraphs 1–12 by reference as though fully set forth herein.

[body.p.32] (normal): 14. Defendant breached the Agreement by doing one or more of the following:

[body.p.33] (normal): a) Failing to maintain the required 8:1 staffing ratio;

[body.p.34] (normal): b) Failing to conduct quarterly reassessments;

[body.p.35] (normal): c) Failing to provide three nutritionally balanced meals of comparable quality;

[body.p.36] (normal): d) Failing to maintain a consistent schedule of social and recreational activities;

[body.p.37] (normal): e) Failing to deliver services outlined in the individualized Care Plan;

[body.p.38] (normal): f) Failing to provide sixty (60) days’ written notice before implementing material changes;

[body.p.39] (normal): g) Failing to maintain service levels reasonably expected under the Agreement.

[body.p.40] (normal): 15. Defendant’s breaches caused Plaintiff to suffer damages, including payment for services not rendered, diminished value of contractual services, and loss of the benefit of the bargain.

[body.p.41] (Heading 2): VI. PRAYER FOR RELIEF

[body.p.42] (normal): Plaintiff respectfully requests that Defendant be cited to appear and answer, and that upon final trial, Plaintiff recover actual damages, attorneys’ fees under Tex. Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code § 38.001, court costs, and all other relief to which she is justly entitled.

[body.p.43] (normal): 
Respectfully submitted,
Plaintiff’s Counsel

[page 4]
IV. FACTUAL ALLEGATIONS
4. Beginning in February 2025, Defendant issued directives from its Chicago headquarters titled
“Operational Sustainability Objectives” and “Flexible Staffing Model.” These directives mandated
reductions in personnel and daily services across facilities nationwide.
5. As a result, staffing ratios at The Grove–Austin increased from 8:1 to as high as 15:1, leaving
residents without adequate care and attention.
6. Defendant also eliminated one of three daily hot meals, substituting a self-serve continental breakfast
for the previously cooked breakfast meal.
7. Finally, Defendant limited its daily wellness and enrichment programs to twice-weekly events,
contrary to the contractual promise of daily engagement.
8. No notice was given to residents or their families regarding these changes, and Defendant continued

91a.2 Contents of Motion.  A motion to dismiss must state that it is made pursuant to this rule, 
must identify each cause of action to which it is addressed, and must state specifically the 
reasons the cause of action has no basis in law, no basis in fact, or both. 
 
91a.3 Time for Motion and Ruling.  A motion to dismiss must be: 
 
(a) 
filed within 60 days after the first pleading containing the challenged cause of 
action  is served on the movant; 
 
(b) 
filed at least 21 days before the motion is heard; and 
 
(c) 
granted or denied within 45 days after the motion is filed. 
 
91a.4 Time for Response.  Any response to the motion must be filed no later than 7 days before 
the date of the hearing. 
 
91a.5 Effect of Nonsuit or Amendment; Withdrawal of Motion. 
 
(a) 
The court may not rule on a motion to dismiss if, at least 3 days before the date of 
the hearing, the respondent files a nonsuit of the challenged cause of action, or the 
movant files a withdrawal of the motion. 
 
(b) 
If the respondent amends the challenged cause of action at least 3 days before the 
date of the hearing, the movant may, before the date of the hearing, file a withdrawal 
of  
the motion or an amended motion directed to the amended cause of action. 
 
(c) 
Except by agreement of the parties, the court must rule on a motion unless it has 
been withdrawn or the cause of action has been nonsuited in accordance with (a) or 
(b).  In ruling on the motion, the court must not consider a nonsuit or amendment 
not filed as permitted by paragraphs (a) or (b). 
 
(d) 
An amended motion filed in accordance with (b) restarts the time periods in this 
rule. 
 
91a.6 Hearing; No Evidence Considered.  Each party is entitled to at least 14 days’ notice of 
the hearing on the motion to dismiss.  The court may, but is not required to, conduct an 
oral hearing on the motion.  Except as required by 91a.7, the court may not consider 
evidence in ruling on the motion and must decide the motion based solely on the pleading 
of the cause of action, together with any pleading exhibits permitted by Rule 59.

[body.p.10] (normal):         	This letter provides a comprehensive legal analysis regarding the ongoing dispute between Susan Whitaker (“Susan”), guardian of The Grove resident Thomas Whitaker (“Thomas”) (collectively, “Whitakers”) and Magnolia Gardens, Inc. (“Magnolia”), specifically concerning a breach of contract claim related to the agreement dated August 1, 2023 between Whitaker and Magnolia (“2023 Agreement”) in the lawsuit filed in Travis County, Texas under Cause No. D-1-GN-25-004732.  As legal counsel representing Magnolia, we have outlined the key issues, defenses, and legal arguments that are likely to arise throughout the case.  Additionally, we have assessed the potential for settlement, evaluated the claim for damages, and provided strategic recommendations for how to proceed.  The goal is to ensure a clear understanding of the strengths and weaknesses of both parties’ positions and to guide the next steps in resolving the dispute in a manner that best serves Magnolia’s interests.

[body.p.13] (normal):  

[body.p.14] (normal): Susan, on behalf of herself and Thomas, originally filed a lawsuit against Magnolia for breach of contract of the 2023 Agreement on May 28, 2025.  Susan later amended her complaint on July 28, 2025, and December 2, 2025.  Her allegations included that core services provisions of the 2023 Agreement (i.e., subsections A, M, and Q of section IV) were breached beginning in February 2025 when policy changes occurred at Magnolia’s facilities as a result of Medicaid and government subsidy reductions.  Specifically, Susan alleges that Thomas experienced gaps in his essential care services due to insufficient staffing, a reduction in breakfast services and scheduled activities, no quarterly reassessments of his care plan, no written notice of such changes, and delays in fee adjustments related to the decrease in services.      

[body.p.15] (normal): The petition does not appear defective, and there do not appear to be any issues with the statute of limitations, venue, forum non conveniens, personal or subject matter jurisdiction, punitive damages, or potential conflicts.

[body.p.18] (normal): On August 1, 2023, Thomas executed the 2023 Agreement with Magnolia that provided the following core services at The Grove, a Magnolia facility located in Austin, Texas:

[body.p.19] (normal):

[body.p.21] (normal):

[Magnolia] shall not materially reduce or eliminate any of the stated services without prior written notice and without providing a corresponding adjustment to the Resident’s fees or alternative services of equal value.  [Magnolia] must provide no less than sixty (60) days’ written notice of any material change affecting services, care, or fees, and must describe the nature of the change, its effective date, and the Resident’s right to terminate this Agreement without penalty before such change takes effect.
  Runs: [body.p.26.r.1]: "Notice of Core Services Changes"(underline)

[body.p.27] (normal):  

[body.p.28] (normal): At the same time, Thomas received a copy of The Grove’s Resident Handbook, stating that 1) the Grove would have a maximum staff-to-resident ratio of 8:1, 2) care plans would be reviewed and updated every 90 days, 3) residents would receive three hot, nutritious meals daily, and 4) a schedule of daily activities would be maintained for the residents.

[body.p.29] (normal):  

[body.p.30] (normal): On August 18, 2023, Thomas’s Initial Resident Care Plan (“Care Plan”) was drafted.  Thomas’s Care Plan required/recommended:  

[body.p.31] (normal):  

[body.p.32] (normal): ·   	Three hot meals daily,

[body.p.33] (normal): ·   	Walking club three times weekly with memory games and music therapy,  and

[body.p.34] (normal): ·   	8:1 staffing ratio or better on all shifts. 

[body.p.35] (normal):  

[body.p.36] (normal): The Care Plan also highlighted that a reassessment was required every 90 days per both Magnolia policy and Texas state regulation.  

[body.p.37] (normal):  

[body.p.38] (normal): In November 2024, you sent an email to your team, noting anticipated funding gaps and proposing cost-saving measures such as adjustments to non-essential programming, a reduction in staff, and changes to meal service formats. 

[body.p.39] (normal):  

[body.p.40] (normal): By January 2025, you alerted staff at all 500 facilities that there would be prompt changes in operations and staffing to accommodate the gaps in Medicaid and government subsidy funding.  You also provided each facility with a Guardian Notification Letter dated January 28, 2025 that would alert all guardians of Magnolia residents to adjustments in staffing, breakfast, and activities beginning February 15, 2025.  However, based on Thomas’s case file, it appears that Susan was never provided such a letter. 

[body.p.41] (normal):  

[body.p.42] (normal): In February 2025, Magnolia changed their Internal Operations manual to reflect the following changes:

[body.p.43] (normal):

[body.p.47] (normal): ·  	Meal requirements were changed from three hot meals daily to two hot meals with dietician oversight eliminated; and

[body.p.48] (normal): ·  	Activity programming requirements were reduced from daily to twice weekly.
